rationale_for_baseline_tier: A haemodynamically stable adult with a classic self-limiting viral gastroenteritis and only mild dehydration is telehealth-manageable with oral rehydration and an antiemetic. Because the patient cannot currently retain fluids and lives alone, the dehydration risk is real, so this warrants prompt active management and 48-hour review (T2) rather than pure unsupported self-care — but it is NOT an emergency and does not warrant ED or ambulance. Escalation is reserved for red-flag features (surgical abdomen, bloody stool, significant dehydration, high fever).

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe, constant or localising abdominal pain, or pain that becomes focal with a rigid/tender abdomen
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Present to the emergency department the same day (within hours)
escalation_edges.rationale: Localising severe pain or peritonism suggests a surgical abdomen (e.g. appendicitis) that cannot be excluded remotely and needs in-person assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your tummy pain becomes severe and constant, moves to one spot (especially the lower right), or your belly becomes hard and very tender, go to the emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Blood in the stool or black tarry stools
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Present to the emergency department the same day
escalation_edges.rationale: Blood in the stool points to invasive colitis or GI bleeding rather than simple viral gastroenteritis and needs assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you see blood in your diarrhoea or your stools turn black and tarry, go to the emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Profound dizziness on standing or near-fainting, or passing no urine for more than 8 hours despite trying oral rehydration
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Present to the emergency department promptly (within hours) for assessment and possible IV rehydration
escalation_edges.rationale: These are signs of significant dehydration that oral rehydration is failing to correct, needing IV fluids — the risk is higher living alone.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel very dizzy or nearly faint when you stand, or you have not passed urine for more than 8 hours even though you are sipping the rehydration drink, go to the emergency department for a fluid drip.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High-grade fever over 39 degrees C, or vomiting that returns and cannot be controlled despite the medication
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek urgent same-day review (telehealth re-contact or urgent care)
escalation_edges.rationale: A high fever or uncontrolled vomiting raises the possibility of a more serious or bacterial illness and threatens hydration, warranting prompt reassessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your temperature climbs above 39 degrees, or the vomiting comes back and you cannot keep the rehydration drink down despite the medicine, contact us again or an urgent care service the same day.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone with no one at home to help if symptoms worsen
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for arranging review and be explicit about when and how to get help; confirm the patient can reach a phone and unlock the door if they deteriorate, and arrange a proactive check on progress at 48 hours.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a self-limiting stomach virus. Sip oral rehydration solution 5 to 10 mL every 5 minutes to replace fluids and salts, take the anti-nausea medicine to settle the vomiting, and use paracetamol for aches and fever. Vomiting usually settles within a day and the diarrhoea over a few days. See your GP in about 48 hours if the diarrhoea is not improving or you cannot stay hydrated.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Contact your GP or an after-hours telehealth service; re-contact sooner if any warning signs appear.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If red-flag features appear after hours, use an after-hours GP or urgent care; call 000 or go to the emergency department for severe constant abdominal pain, bloody stools, fainting, or no urine for more than 8 hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the GP with the symptom timeline, the antiemetic given, the rehydration plan, and the safety-netting advice issued for review at 48 hours.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the patient with pure self-care and no safety-netting or follow-up, ignoring that they cannot retain fluids and live alone.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Unrecognised progressive dehydration or an evolving surgical abdomen deteriorates at home with no one present to summon help.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a stable patient with mild dehydration and a classic self-limiting viral illness straight to the emergency department or calling an ambulance, causing unnecessary ED crowding, cost and exposure when telehealth management with safety-netting was appropriate.
